preparation_method: raw seafood cubed and arranged over sushi rice
tamago (Japanese egg omelette) prepared by pan-frying
tiger prawn served as garnish topping
allergens: soy (likely in sushi rice seasoning and tamago)
gluten (likely in tamago and soy-based seasonings)

serving.natural_language_portion: One upsized serving bowl containing approximately 450 g total, comprising roughly 220 g sushi rice and 230 g of mixed seafood toppings including cubed salmon, tuna, shrimp, ikura, tamago, and a tiger prawn.

nutrient_source.title: Estimated from USDA FoodData Central component foods: Sushi rice (cooked, vinegared), raw salmon, raw tuna, cooked shrimp, salmon roe (ikura), Japanese egg omelette (tamago), and cooked tiger prawn; weighted composite per described portion.
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central (Foundation Foods and SR Legacy)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single approved-source entry exists for bara chirashi don. Nutrient values are estimated by weighted composite of USDA FoodData Central entries for each component: sushi rice (~49% by weight), raw Atlantic salmon (~18%), raw yellowfin tuna (~11%), cooked shrimp (~9%), salmon roe/ikura (~5%), tamago/Japanese egg omelette (~5%), and tiger prawn (~3%). Per-100g values derived from the blended composite. Energy cross-checked using Atwater factors.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson FS, Brand-Miller JC, Foster-Powell K, Buyken AE, Goletzke J; and University of Sydney GI Database entry for sushi / vinegared rice.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for bara chirashi don. GI estimated at 48 based on the University of Sydney GI Database entry for sushi/vinegared rice (GI ~48–55, mean ~48; Atkinson et al. 2021, Am J Clin Nutr). Vinegared sushi rice is the dominant glycemic component (~49% by weight). Seafood toppings contribute negligible glycemic load. Composite GI weighted toward sushi rice GI of 48. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (48 × 83.25) / 100 ≈ 40.0 g (serving carbohydrate: 18.5 g/100g × 450 g serving = 83.25 g).

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content (~210 mg/100 g; ~945 mg per upsized serving) from sushi rice seasoning, soy-based condiments, and seafood
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should be aware that one upsized serving provides approximately 945 mg sodium (~39% of the 2400 mg daily limit), and should limit additional soy sauce or sodium-containing condiments at the table.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic load (~40 per upsized serving) driven by vinegared sushi rice; high-quality protein from seafood supports satiety
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should note the moderate glycemic load of approximately 40 per upsized serving; portion control of the rice component and pairing with the high-protein seafood toppings can help moderate postprandial glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Rich in omega-3 fatty acids (EPA and DHA) from salmon, tuna, ikura, and shrimp; low saturated fat (~0.8 g/100 g)
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The high omega-3 fatty acid content from multiple oily fish components (salmon, tuna, ikura) is cardioprotective; however, individuals with cardiovascular disease should monitor sodium intake from this dish and avoid additional high-sodium condiments.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Upsized Bara Chirashi Don is a Japanese rice bowl dish consisting of approximately 230 g of assorted cubed raw seafood — including salmon, tuna, shrimp, and ikura (salmon roe) — along with tamago (Japanese sweet egg omelette), all arranged over a generous bed of vinegared sushi rice and topped with a whole tiger prawn. 'Bara' (バラ) means 'scattered' or 'loose', distinguishing this style from the more formal layered chirashi-zushi; 'don' (丼) refers to the rice bowl format. The dish is served chilled or at room temperature. The flavour profile is clean, umami-rich, and lightly tangy from the vinegared rice, with the sweetness of tamago and the briny pop of ikura providing contrast. The texture combines the soft, slightly sticky sushi rice with the silky raw fish, the firm bite of shrimp and prawn, and the yielding egg omelette. Nutritionally, this dish is a high-protein, moderate-carbohydrate meal rich in omega-3 fatty acids (EPA and DHA) from salmon, tuna, and ikura, making it beneficial for cardiovascular health. It provides significant vitamin B12, selenium, and vitamin D from the seafood components. The glycemic index is low-to-moderate (~48) due to the acetic acid in vinegared rice slowing starch digestion. Sodium content is moderate and primarily from rice seasoning and natural seafood sodium. Clinically, it is a nutritionally dense meal suitable for most adults when consumed in appropriate portions, with attention to sodium for hypertensive individuals and glycemic load for those managing Type 2 Diabetes.

confidence_reason: No single approved-source entry exists for bara chirashi don as a composite dish. Nutrient values are estimated by weighted composite of USDA FoodData Central Foundation Foods and SR Legacy entries for each identified component ingredient. The description from the input record specifies 230 g of seafood toppings and a tiger prawn over sushi rice, allowing a reasonably defensible weight-based composite. GI is estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for vinegared sushi rice as the dominant glycemic component. Confidence is medium rather than high because no direct measurement of this specific dish exists in any approved primary source.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are composite estimates derived from USDA FoodData Central component entries; no direct approved-source measurement of bara chirashi don exists. 2. Serving size of 450 g is estimated: 230 g seafood toppings (as stated in input description) plus approximately 220 g sushi rice (typical for a Japanese rice bowl serving). 3. trans_fat_g is 0.0 — confirmed zero for raw seafood and vinegared rice components per USDA FoodData Central; naturally zero for these minimally processed marine and grain ingredients. 4. monounsaturated_fat_g (-1 sentinel not used; value estimated at 1.1 g from USDA component data for salmon and tuna). 5. polyunsaturated_fat_g estimated at 1.1 g, reflecting omega-3 and omega-6 content from salmon, tuna, and ikura. 6. GI of 48 is an analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database sushi/vinegared rice data (Atkinson et al. 2021); no direct GI measurement for bara chirashi don exists. 7. Glycemic load per serving ~40 is calculated as (48 × 83.25 g carbohydrate per 450 g serving) / 100. 8. Allergen profile includes fish, shellfish, egg, and likely soy and gluten from tamago preparation and sushi rice seasoning; halal status is not confirmed as raw seafood sourcing and preparation standards are unknown. 9. The cuisine_tag 'Alcoholic Drink' in the input source data appears to be a field survey platform tagging artefact and has been disregarded as it does not describe this food. 10. All -1 values in per_100g fields (monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g) have been replaced with estimated numeric values from USDA component data; no -1 sentinel values remain in per_100g.
